I watch them grow. They're not much more than I ever knew. And I think to myself what I wonder for my life. Yes, I think to myself what I wonder for my life. Oh yeah.

segments.text: Hi, this is Ed Crainpool, formerly the New York Metz X Metz for over 40 years and I've been in my own business.
I are in payment systems and I'm listening to Jimmy Scott's FI and Type.
And now the purple dusk of twilight time steals across the metals of my heart.
I have been the sky, the little stars climb, always reminding me that we're apart.
You wander down the lane and far away, leaving me a song that will not die.
Love is now the star dust of yesterday, the music of the years gone by.
We're listening to Jimmy Scott's high and tight presented by a sponsor, Tubby Name, later.
And today we have Monty Irvin on the other line.
Monty Irvin was elected to the baseball hall of fame in 1973.
He is actually a member of... Did you realize that you're a member of nine different halls of fame in the US, Mexico, Cuba and Puerto Rico?
Yeah. That's a pretty good accomplishment, I think.
Just delighted.
Yeah, I remember.
I'm sure. And you also wrote your own autobiography with James Riley in 1996. Nice guys finish first.
How did you write that book? Did James Riley approach you or was it something you'd wanted to do when you approached him?
Yeah, well I had already always wanted to leave the other people and know something about me.
And Jim Rowley suggested that I cooperate with him and we did the best job we could.
Did you have the... Were you under the impression that a lot of people had forgotten about you?
Well, you know, I wasn't a... I came up late. I wasn't a household name.
I had maybe six or seven of you on good years. And you know, after you quit playing, people forget pretty quickly.
And so I wanted to, you know, something that my children and grandchildren could read after I'm gone.
That's what prompted me to do that.
Now your career was over after the 1956 season. And why did it end? Did you have back problems I saw?
Yeah, I tried to play and I played four or five innings. And I was like, we're good stuff.
And then I took my whole day to recoup and I get out to next day and try to play again the same thing that would happen.
And so I saw an orthopedic surgeon up in Los Angeles and he jammed on me and said that, you know, I had a bad back.
And he wouldn't suggest an operation because the success was only perfect, perfect.
And he said, out of baseball, it wouldn't affect me. And indeed that's what's happened. You know, it hadn't slowed me down.
It had margin. It hadn't bothered me out of baseball.
I know after, I believe in 1957, you tried to come back. You played in the minor leagues, I think?
Yeah, I played in Los Angeles in the angels, I think. In Los Angeles, a farm club, I was being a cub.
And I just couldn't do it.
Couldn't do it. So I retired.
How did you feel when you were, because you had been, because you started playing when you were 17 years old, you were...
In high school, you would just started college when you started to play professional ball with him.
I was still in high school.
Okay. And because I know you had an alias, your name was Jimmy Nelson, you had to call yourself.
Yeah, that was because I didn't want to lose my am, I was just standing.
And I was able to play a couple of years without losing that standing.
And then, in 1939, I stopped going to college and what was the ego full time?
Where did you come up with the name Jimmy Nelson?
For the line, I played against. He was a catcher. I started out as a catcher. He had a great physique.
And I looked good in his uniform, and I looked good for that good catcher. Good hitter, good throw.
He was my like, hostly hero. So I just took his name.
He must have been flattered as the years went on.
Well, after I did it, I never did see me anymore. And I wonder if he's still alive.
I know he lived right there in East Orange. And I lived in Orange.
But I never get chance to see him anymore or talk to him.
And he could still be alive someplace. I just don't know.
So if he is alive, I wish he could contact me so that we could talk and compare those.
And he was a couple of years older than you or was he?
Yeah, well, yeah.
Okay, because as we speak now, it's 2000 and the 2009 and you're 90 years old right now.
Yeah. So you've been...
That's me and this 90 still.
Okay. Because you've been retired now for 63 years.
That's... because you played basically 20 years of professional ball.
You were in the Negro leagues from 1938 to 48.
And then 49 to 56, you were in the major leagues.
So it's... you know, 20 years of baseball, but 60 plus outside of baseball.
Yeah.
Not on the field.
When you were done with the game in 1957 and you looked back at your 20 years in professional baseball,
where you played all over the world. I mean, you played in Cuba and Mexico and Puerto Rico as well as the US.
What were some of the things that you would look back on and we're especially proud of?
Well, that gave me a chance to visit these countries.
Please, the fans, particularly in Puerto Rico, Cuba and Mexico.
And, you know, got a chance to earn a little bit more money by the way out of the country.
And make some lifelong friends.
And they will save a few bucks.
That's the reason why, you know, well, I look back at that and I'm grateful for it.
I recently read a book about a statue page.
And you were mentioned a number times in the book and it talked a lot about the lifestyle that the Negro leagueers had, especially in the 30s,
in which you would be born storming and you'd play one game in one town in maybe the morning or...
And then you'd be able to drive an hour or so and then play another full game the same day.
You did a lot of that?
We did quite a bit of that.
Were you playing baseball year round? You were going to the winter leagues?
Yeah, for the most part.
Seeing when it may be the middle of September, sometimes in the end of September in the States.
And then we'd go to Cuba, Mexico, Puerto Rico, you know, Dominican Republic, or Venezuela.
So I was able to play in three of those countries.
And it was delightful.
Again, I made a little bit more money and got me away from the cold winter zone of the North.
That was good.
And usually the season of how the country would...
What year maybe, first or second week in September, you would rest up maybe three or four weeks and ready to go again.
And when you're young, you know, you could cook real quick.
And that was, you know, suppression and all.
You get enough rest to perform in the following summer without getting too tired.
So a lot of players were doing it.
And I was no different.
I just did the same thing, same as they were doing.
Now is the racism not as bad in those other countries in Cuba?
Yes, yes.
Much less than in the United States and in the American country.
Particularly in Mexico.
Really enjoyed playing in Mexico because I played there in 1942.
I hit 397.
But I didn't home run.
I was in the trouble ground by two RBI's.
And I was the most likely player.
I really felt free for a time.
And my life, I felt free going anywhere, doing anything anybody else there.
Staying in a hotel, eating any restaurant, go to any theater.
And the people was just wonderful.
I really enjoyed it.
And since then, I've been back a few times to visit and to feel like great, wonderful feeling.
So I had one I played there.
That openness that was there for you in especially Mexico.
Was that really just, I guess, just a cultural reason?
Why was it so different in Mexico from the United States?
I don't want to have any segregation laws.
Now everybody was, again, equal when color wasn't a factor.
And, you know, when you open society and you don't feel like a segregation or a segregation,
it's a real joy.
So I enjoyed it.
Really enjoyed it.
Yes, very fun.
It was that beer in Mexico.
Now you left, actually, you were with the norquic Eagles and you left the Eagles and you went and played in Mexico.
Why did you leave?
I left because I had a plan on getting married and it wasn't making enough money with the Eagles to support a wife.
And I got the offer from Joyce Pascal, who was the father of baseball in Mexico.
And he paid me four times as much as I was making and it was the Eagles.
So I took off and I left.
But my honeymoon down there, wonderful country, had just been rebuilt from a previous earthquake.
Again, you know, new feel, new-wise, ever streets, new feel, brand new feel, when I was there.
You know, open and it was dedicated to most beautiful women.
And in Mexico, was there doing a bullfight season, had never seen a bullfight.
And so, what we would do, we started games maybe 10 o'clock in the morning, so we'd be finished.
And so that we could attend the bullfight at one o'clock.
And then after the game or after the bullfight, you know, go out to dinner and really enjoy, you know, have a good time.
When I was playing there, I was playing lots of Ben, maybe you had to see, you know, spend eight or ten an hour on American players playing.
And we'd see all get together, you know, especially on certain days or certain events would happen.
But you know, Christmas time, you know, man.
And you know, we sing songs and sing at dance. It was just wonderful.
Don't know why there's no sun up in the sky, starving with her.
Since my man and I ate together, keeps raining all the time.
Oh yeah, life is bad.
Glument is red everywhere, stormy weather, stormy weather.
And I just can't get my poor self together.
Oh, I'm weary all the time.
The time.
That was the 1942 season and then right after that, or the 22 year.
And then you got drafted because World War II was going on.
Yeah, then I went home and first of all, I went home, got a job.
And then March of the next year, March 9, 1943, I was inducted into the US in other states army.
And it goes to change my whole life.
We reported a fort digs then on to camp right outside of Washington, D.C.
And then later on, we went on down to TMD down to Camp Cleveland, Louisiana.
And then from there, we went back up to Boston in January 1, 1944.
We all loaded a ship.
There was a British cruise ship.
And for 19 days, we sail in the Nell thick Atlantic.
And after 19 days landed in Liverpool, England, which is, you know,
we were part of a 500 ship convoy.
And we went, you know, we were part of that convoy.
And I say to you to say that, out of 500 ships, not one would fall.
And then it was quite an event, quite odd, was what's so enjoyable, of course, about the safe and sound.
Were you nervous during those 19 days knowing where you were?
Yeah, you know.
Not Atlantic is not even peacetime in the North Atlantic doing a winter.
It's quite an event.
You know, we're going to get an iceberg or something, or something, or whatever.
But none of that happened.
And we were delighted that it didn't.
Because you were in the water, maybe, two or three minutes.
You could not have survived because it was so cold.
The seas were so rough.
So we just had just delighted, nothing happened, but we're able to get to Liverpool safe and sound.
And then were you sent to France after that, after D-Day, I believe?
Yeah. And then we stayed there from January until August and England.
And then D-Day was June 6, 1944.
Two months later, June 1st, we landed in Omaha Beach.
The beach had been cleared. The road had been open.
And we didn't have much of a problem.
When we got to France, our first duties was to guard the Nazi prisoners.
And we built roads and did a lot of manual jobs.
And so that was the way it was until the war ended.
And you were in a battalion that was not integrated, right?
It was still a segregated.
No, of course it was segregated.
It was segregated.
Everything was unequal.
And we want to treat it very well.
Not by the only myself, but by the only soldiers.
Here we are in a place where we're trying to fight against the brutal situation and not Germany.
And we were in the army.
I think that was hard to understand.
But somehow we survived it.
And kept at home, you know, except from the first.
So during those...
Back home, safe and sound.
And during those three years were you thinking much about baseball?
No.
No, we're in England.
We played a little self-ball once and a while.
It was all the hard to get back.
And you know, the climate wasn't a good either.
And then after we got to France, we were busy.
Really busy because, you know, the invasion has started.
And we were just too busy to play.
Again, once and a while we might give a self-ball game.
Somebody would call it a ball.
But the end of it.
But the extent of it.
I didn't get a chance to play baseball for three years.
And when I got back to the States, being young, of course, didn't take long to get back in a groove.
And try to recapture that old feeling.
So that was what it was.
We had a little basketball, a little basketball, a football, a kick-to-ball ball.
But we didn't have chance to play in the baseball.
Now, before you were drafted, the owners of the Negro Leagues had chosen you to be...
I heard that later, yes.
So you had no idea that they chose you to be the guy to break the color line.
You know, you have to hit him running.
If you only throw him in the fire.
They said I was second and none.
And I had to temper him out, of course, too.
You know, to get along with people, players, ownership, and so on.
But, actually, 45, Jackie signed with Ricky.
Frank Ricky.
And I got a chance to sign with.
I told him that I wasn't ready to report.
I didn't ready to.
I'd like to defer my beginning until I, you know, fell up to it.
I said, well, when you get...
When you feel like it, just let us know.
And we'll, you know, and we'll sign you.
So it happened in 1949 in Cuba.
Alex Pompeo, Scott Pooley, Scott Pooley, put a giant,
said that the harsh stone on our giant wanted to send it.
I told my hangtons, and I did.
And then we reported that spring into Sanford, Florida as a member of the New York Giant.
Rest of his history, of course.
Were you ever upset or did you ever regret the fact that just the timing didn't work out?
And you couldn't be the first...
You thought about a little better, but I didn't dwell on it.
You know, Jackie had been signed.
He was doing a good job, did a good job.
And even though he wasn't the best player, he was the most adequate to do the job that he did.
He constantly improved, became a real good second baseman.
He became a clutch hitter. He became very thrilling on the basis.
And he did a good job.
He was very eloquent, you know, good speaker, college educator, and so on.
So no one could complain about the job that he did.
We're proud of, we're, you know, the land biggest of his position.
But, you know, well, we weren't jealous.
Oh, it was his worst and the best of luck and his succeed.
It was a great job of pioneering.
What were the differences between you and Jackie Robinson as people and as players?
Oh, I don't know. You know, he's a player, I could play.
You mentioned temperament. Did you add a different temperament than maybe he had?
No, he was a little feisty.
You know, he had flare ups.
I was more calm, cool, and collected.
I was able to get along with, you know, my teammates looked better and he was.
But sometimes it's good to be feisty.
So all in all, I can't complain or anyone can complain about the job that he did.
He got a lot of publicity and he lived up to the headlines.
And he just did an overall great job of pioneering to be the first African-American to break into the major leagues.
How did you feel?
You came up in 1949 and not too long after because a lot of the big stars then started signing into the major leagues.
The Negro leagues really started to wane and eventually they went bankrupt and went out of business.
How did you feel? Were you paying much attention to that?
Well, we know that if Jackie succeeded and they started to sign other players,
that the demise of Negro leagues would be over.
That they would have to go out of business.
Everybody realized that.
Owners knew it, players knew it, everybody.
And so I was just, you know, the price you had for people for progress.
So, you know, I played the Negro league, I played it as long as they could.
I was going nationally, of course, officially ended.
And in 1950, started in 1920 and officially ended in 1950.
So he goes in 1948, moved from New York, New Jersey to Houston, Texas.
And then went out of business.
In the New York league, they continued a few years more.
Of course, they were traveling all over.
And they still could go here and there.
And people have not seen them play.
So they continue for four or five years.
But it was not like it, you know, it had been the East West game, of course, ended.
And, you know, baseball was now integrated.
And you know, the owners started to sign the best players they could play.
That's the way it was, and that's progress.
We realized that.
I tried so not to give in.
I said to myself, this affair never will go so well.
And why should I try to resist when darling, my nose so well?
I've got you under my skin.
I'd sacrifice anything, come what might, while the sake of having you near,
In spite of a warning voice that comes in the night, and repeats, and repeats in my ear.
Don't you know it or who?
You never can win.
Use your mentality, wake up to reality.
But each time I do, just the thought of you makes me stop before I begin.
Because I've got you.
Oh yes, I've got you.
But you.
I do my skin.
By the time your rookie season with the Giants began in 1949, you were a veteran.
You'd been playing for a number of years.
You'd been in the Negro leagues.
You'd been in Cuba and Mexico, like we mentioned, in Puerto Rico.
You'd been in a veteran of World War II.
When you began playing in the major leagues, were you apprehensive or nervous about that?
Or what were some of the things that you noticed were different from your comp?
You were a little apprehensive.
Sorry that I hadn't been given a chance 10 years earlier.

Don Nookam even learned over there.

Played well.
Most of us played.

And one of my usual pages was 42.
We were in 1948 when we got a chance to sign with Cleveland and Indigan.

He made an awesome record.
And of course there was, you know, I'm talking about the real stars.
I'm talking about Willie Wells and Ray Dandridge and John Henry Lloyd and Oscar Charles.
Now these kind of colors, cool pop of bell, he kind of fell off.
They were, you know, truly super stars.
And most of them just missed it totally.
We were fortunate, you know, we came in late, but we did, you know, we formed the best we could.
And most of the time that wasn't too bad.
And you guys had a harder time than your white teammates as well.
Again, the travel wasn't the best.
Yeah, yeah, one of the way the country was, you know, the situation and the question of the country,
there's still some, you know, some of the people still here in this country right now,
still fighting the Civil War.
But, you know, we, we, the worst we heard in the Cleveland, we received, we ever, you know,
we kind of used to, you know, you never get used to it, but we knew how to handle ourselves.
And that was a big thing, trying to do a good job in an unequal or uneven situation.
And then, you know, I performed well.
And I would say we did a good job in that regard.
And you, and your era really helped pave the way for civil rights in this country.
And for, for basically our president right now, Barack Obama, you don't start it back there.
That's true, yeah, yeah.
You know, we all, we all made contributions in our own way.
A lot of the youngsters, a lot of the players who, who could march and so on, supported with money.
And then, at the time, so, we all tried to do the best we could to make a bad situation better.
That's what I like to say.
And 17 years after you retired in 1973, you were elected to the baseball hall of fame.
Why did it take 17 years?
Well, you know, you have to, you have to sit down, and you have to be at least out of age for five years before you're eligible.
Okay, so there's a long list.
And sometimes it takes, you know, that long, you know, to be recognized.
And then, way back in 1970, Commissioner Bowie came, and I went to the baseball commissioners office as a finish baseball.
I worked as a special assistant to a commissioner Bowie Kim.
He asked me how a former committee to try to honor those players who never got a chance to play.

Second, and third person picked was Josh Gibson and Bucklington.
And of course, Cool Papa Bell, DeHego, Martin DeHego, Judy Johnson, Ronald Down the Line.
So, it took a while.
You know, baseball, hierarchy.
I guess they knew, but didn't want to, you know, believe that there were so many real good players that were good enough to play in a major.
They were all the famous, all the famous talent.
So, it took them a lot to realize that after the rickets were, you know, researched and brought out, that these players indeed belong.
So, it took a while.
You know, it didn't matter how long it took, as long as the job got done, that's what I'm trying to say.
And so, you were involved with the integration of the major leagues on the field and also into the Hall of Fame.
You involved them both, no.
You were a scout for the New York Metz in 1967 and 1968.
Between those 11 years, between, or 10 years, between 1957, when you really retired.
In 1967, when you started scouting for the Metz, what did you do for those 10 years? Did you have a job?
I worked for local brewery.
A famous brewery, a New York brewery, by the name of Rango.
You know, by beer, it's Rango, the dry beer. For them, for 11 years.
And it was a good job. I had two daughters in college, so I had to pay for their education.
And as I started working for them, I found I was not a bad job.
And I worked, you know, from 1957 up until 1968.
And then when Commissioner Boyd June came in, I started working for him.
From 1969 to 1984. 1984, I turned 65.
So I was a retired and moved to Florida.
I lived in Florida for 22 years. And my wife passed on.
So I have a daughter, son-in-law, daughter in Houston, and two granddaughters.
So there's a dad come on over here near us, so we can kind of look after you.
And that's the situation now. So they look after me very well.
I'm very well attended to and happy.
I tried to live as long as I can. Come February 2nd of April, I'll be 91.
And that's how I reached my 91st birthday. I hope to live a few more years.
I just, you know, one day at a time. That's the way it is.
When was the last time you picked up a baseball or a bat or a glove?
I was running a long time. It's been at least 20 years. It used to go out.

And I used to play in some of the old climbers game.

You can't do that. You say, well, it's time to quit.
So all I do now, I go to the stadium, I watch a baseball on TV,
and I'm very happy just doing that. I still enjoy the greatest game in my estimation.
It's ever been in baseball.
Who I should stay away, but what can I do? I hear your name.
